meta.title: API Versioning & Evolution
meta.description: Review checklist for API Versioning & Evolution projects

items.text: Are existing fields being removed or renamed without a deprecation period?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Removing or renaming a field in an API response is a breaking change. Existing clients that depend on that field will break immediately upon deployment.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consumers cannot update their integrations instantly. Removing fields without warning causes runtime errors in production for downstream services and mobile apps that haven't updated.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Compare the API schema diff for removed or renamed fields
- Check if a deprecation notice was added before removal
- Verify the field isn't referenced in active client SDKs
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'username' field is being removed from the /users response. This will break all clients that reference it. Deprecate it first and add a sunset timeline.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// v1 response: { "username": "alice", "email": "..." }
// v2 response: { "email": "..." }  ← username removed without warning

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never remove fields without a deprecation period. Add the replacement alongside the old field first.

items.text: Are field types being changed in a way that breaks existing parsers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changing a field's type (e.g., string to integer, object to array) is a breaking change even if the field name stays the same. Clients with typed schemas will fail to deserialize.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Type changes cause deserialization failures, type errors, and data corruption in statically-typed clients. Even dynamically-typed clients may break if they perform type-specific operations.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check schema diffs for type changes on existing fields
- Verify backward compatibility by testing with the previous client SDK
- Look for changes from singular to array or vice versa
baseContent.exampleComment: Changing 'tags' from a comma-separated string to an array of strings will break every client that does string splitting. Add a new field 'tagList' instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Before: { "tags": "bug,urgent" }
// After:  { "tags": ["bug", "urgent"] }  ← type changed

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// Keep old field, add new one:
{ "tags": "bug,urgent", "tagList": ["bug", "urgent"] }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never change a field's type in-place. Add a new field with the desired type and deprecate the old one.

items.text: Are new required parameters being added to existing endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Adding a required parameter to an existing endpoint forces all clients to update their requests. Clients that don't include the new parameter will receive 400 errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Existing integrations stop working immediately. This is especially harmful for mobile apps where users may not update for weeks or months.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for new required fields in request schemas
- Verify new parameters have sensible defaults that preserve existing behavior
- Look for validation changes that reject previously valid requests
baseContent.exampleComment: The new 'region' parameter is required, but existing clients don't send it. Make it optional with a default value that preserves current behavior.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: New parameters on existing endpoints must be optional with backward-compatible defaults.

items.text: Are endpoint URLs or HTTP methods being changed without redirects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changing an endpoint's URL path or HTTP method is a breaking change. Clients hardcode these into their request logic.
baseContent.whyItMatters: URL changes cause 404 errors for all existing clients. Without redirects, there's no graceful migration path.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for renamed or moved routes in the diff
- Verify 301/308 redirects are set up from old paths to new ones
- Confirm the old endpoint continues to work during the transition period
baseContent.exampleComment: The endpoint moved from /api/users to /api/v2/accounts. Add a 308 redirect from the old path so existing clients continue to work.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: When moving endpoints, keep the old URL working with a permanent redirect to the new location.

items.text: Are error response formats being changed in a way that breaks client error handling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Changing the structure of error responses (field names, nesting, error codes) breaks client-side error handling logic that parses these responses.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clients often build retry logic, user-facing messages, and error tracking around specific error response formats. Changing them silently degrades error handling.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Compare error response schemas before and after the change
- Check if error codes or machine-readable identifiers are preserved
- Verify client SDKs can still parse the new error format
baseContent.exampleComment: The error response changed from { error: string } to { errors: Error[] }. Clients parsing the 'error' field will get undefined. Keep both formats during migration.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Error response formats are part of the API contract. Evolve them with the same care as success responses.

items.text: Do deprecated endpoints include Sunset and Deprecation headers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: RFC 8594 defines the Sunset header to communicate when an endpoint will be removed. The Deprecation header signals that an endpoint is deprecated but still functional.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without machine-readable deprecation signals, clients have no automated way to detect they're using soon-to-be-removed endpoints. Headers enable tooling to flag these proactively.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that deprecated endpoints set the Deprecation header with a date
- Verify the Sunset header is set with the removal date
- Confirm the dates are in IMF-fixdate format (RFC 7231)
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint is marked as deprecated in the docs but doesn't send Sunset or Deprecation headers. Add them so API clients and monitoring tools can detect the deprecation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Sunset and Deprecation headers to give clients machine-readable deprecation notices.

items.text: Is there a reasonable deprecation timeline before removal?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A deprecation timeline defines how long deprecated features remain available before removal. It gives consumers time to migrate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Too-short timelines force emergency migrations. Too-long timelines create maintenance burden. The timeline should match consumer update cycles (e.g., mobile apps need longer than web apps).
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the deprecation announcement includes a removal date
- Verify the timeline is at least one release cycle (typically 3-6 months)
- Confirm migration documentation exists before the deprecation is announced
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint is deprecated with a 2-week sunset. Given that mobile clients may take weeks to update, extend the timeline to at least 90 days.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set deprecation timelines based on your slowest consumer's update cycle. 90 days is a common minimum.

items.text: Is usage of deprecated endpoints being tracked to inform removal decisions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before removing a deprecated endpoint, you should know how many clients still use it and how often. Usage metrics inform whether it's safe to proceed with removal.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Removing an endpoint that still receives significant traffic causes widespread outages. Monitoring lets you reach out to remaining consumers before the sunset date.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that deprecated endpoints have usage logging or metrics
- Verify dashboards or alerts exist for deprecated endpoint traffic
- Confirm there's a process for contacting active consumers
baseContent.exampleComment: This deprecated endpoint is being removed, but there's no logging to track remaining usage. Add metrics before proceeding with removal.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Just removing the route
// app.get('/v1/legacy', handler);  ← deleted

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Track deprecated endpoint usage. Don't remove endpoints that still have active consumers without outreach.

items.text: Does the deprecation notice point to the replacement endpoint or migration guide?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A deprecation notice should tell consumers not just that something is going away, but what to use instead. The Link header with rel=successor-version is the standard approach.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without clear migration instructions, consumers either ignore the deprecation or waste time figuring out the replacement. This increases support burden and delays migration.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that deprecated endpoints include a Link header pointing to the replacement
- Verify API documentation includes migration instructions
- Confirm changelogs reference the deprecation and replacement
baseContent.exampleComment: The deprecation notice says 'use the new endpoint' but doesn't specify which one. Add a Link header with the successor URL and update the API docs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: { "warning": "This endpoint is deprecated" }

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: { "warning": "This endpoint is deprecated. Use /v2/users instead. See https://docs.example.com/migration/v1-to-v2" }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every deprecation notice must include a pointer to the replacement and migration instructions.

items.text: Are client SDKs updated to reflect deprecations before the API changes ship?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When an API endpoint is deprecated, official client SDKs should mark the corresponding methods as deprecated and provide alternatives. This gives IDE users instant feedback.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SDK users won't check API changelogs. Compiler/IDE deprecation warnings are the most effective way to reach them before the sunset date.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that SDK methods for deprecated endpoints are marked with @deprecated annotations
- Verify the SDK version with deprecation warnings is published before the API sunset
- Confirm the SDK's changelog mentions the deprecation
baseContent.exampleComment: The /v1/search endpoint is deprecated in the API, but the SDK's search() method has no deprecation annotation. Add @deprecated with a migration note.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Ship SDK deprecation warnings before the API sunset so developers get IDE-level feedback.

items.text: Is the versioning strategy (URL path, header, query param) consistent across all endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: APIs can be versioned via URL path (/v1/), custom header (X-API-Version), Accept header (application/vnd.api.v1+json), or query parameter (?version=1). Mixing strategies creates confusion.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent versioning means clients need different logic for different endpoints. It also makes it harder to build middleware, documentation, and routing.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that all endpoints use the same versioning mechanism
- Verify new endpoints follow the established pattern
- Confirm versioning documentation is accurate
baseContent.exampleComment: Most endpoints use URL-path versioning (/v2/), but this new endpoint uses a header. Align with the existing strategy for consistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick one versioning strategy and use it everywhere. URL-path versioning is the most common and discoverable.

items.text: Can old and new API versions run simultaneously during migration?

baseContent.whatItMeans: During migration, both API versions should be available so consumers can switch at their own pace. This requires the infrastructure to route to the correct handler based on version.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If old and new versions can't coexist, migration becomes a big-bang cutover with high risk of outages. Parallel operation enables gradual, low-risk migration.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Verify both old and new endpoint versions are deployed simultaneously
- Check that database schema changes are backward-compatible with both versions
- Confirm shared resources (caches, queues) work with both versions
baseContent.exampleComment: The v2 migration changes the database schema in a way that breaks v1 queries. Add a backward-compatible view or adapter so v1 continues to work during migration.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design migrations so both API versions can operate simultaneously against the same data store.

items.text: Are response transformations between versions handled by a dedicated layer?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple API versions return different shapes of the same data, transformation logic should live in a dedicated mapping layer rather than being scattered across handlers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inline transformations in each handler lead to duplicated, inconsistent logic. A centralized transformation layer is easier to test and maintain.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if version-specific response shaping is isolated in middleware or mapper functions
- Look for duplicated transformation logic across version-specific handlers
- Verify transformations are unit-tested
baseContent.exampleComment: Both v1 and v2 handlers query the same service but manually shape the response differently. Extract a shared mapper so the transformation logic is in one place.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Centralize version-specific response transformations in a dedicated mapper layer.

items.text: Are there automated tests verifying both API versions produce correct responses?

baseContent.whatItMeans: During the migration period, both old and new API versions must produce correct responses. Automated contract tests verify this without manual testing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without automated tests for both versions, regressions in the old version go undetected. This is especially dangerous because the old version receives less attention during migration.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for contract tests covering both v1 and v2 endpoints
- Verify test suites run against both versions in CI
- Look for snapshot tests or schema validation for response shapes
baseContent.exampleComment: The test suite only covers v2 endpoints. Add contract tests for v1 to catch regressions during the migration period.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Maintain automated tests for all supported API versions throughout the migration period.

items.text: Is there a step-by-step migration guide for consumers upgrading between versions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A migration guide documents every change between versions with before/after examples, making it straightforward for consumers to update their integrations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a clear guide, consumers reverse-engineer changes from trial and error, increasing support requests and slowing migration.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that a migration guide exists for each major version bump
- Verify it includes field mapping tables (old name → new name)
- Confirm code examples show before and after for common operations
baseContent.exampleComment: The v1→v2 migration changes 12 field names and 3 endpoint URLs, but there's no migration guide. Create one with a mapping table and example requests.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Publish a detailed migration guide with before/after examples for every breaking version change.

items.text: Is there a rollback plan if the new version causes unexpected issues?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A rollback plan defines how to revert to the previous API version if the new version causes problems. This includes database compatibility, feature flags, and routing changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a rollback plan, a broken new version leads to extended outages. Having a tested rollback procedure reduces mean time to recovery.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the deployment plan includes rollback steps
- Verify database migrations are reversible
- Confirm feature flags or routing rules can redirect traffic back to the old version
baseContent.exampleComment: The v2 deployment includes an irreversible database migration. This means we can't roll back if v2 has issues. Consider making the migration additive-only.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always have a tested rollback plan. Prefer additive database changes that are compatible with both versions.

items.text: Are changes additive-only (new fields, new endpoints) rather than modifying existing ones?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Additive changes — adding new fields, endpoints, or optional parameters — are backward-compatible by definition. Modifying or removing existing elements is not.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Additive-only evolution eliminates the need for version bumps for most changes, reducing migration burden for consumers.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that existing fields are unchanged in the schema diff
- Verify new fields have sensible defaults and are optional
- Confirm no existing behavior is altered by the additions
baseContent.exampleComment: Instead of changing the 'status' field to include new values, add a 'detailedStatus' field. Existing clients can ignore it while new clients benefit from the richer data.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Modifying existing field's possible values
{ "status": "processing_payment" }  // was: "processing"

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// Additive: keep old field, add new one
{ "status": "processing", "statusDetail": "processing_payment" }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer additive changes over modifications. Add new fields rather than changing existing ones.

items.text: Are clients expected to ignore unknown fields (forward compatibility)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Forward compatibility means clients should tolerate unknown fields in responses rather than failing. This lets the server add fields without breaking older clients.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If clients have strict parsing that rejects unknown fields, every server-side addition becomes a breaking change. Tolerant readers enable safe API evolution.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check client SDK deserialization settings for strict vs. lenient parsing
- Verify API documentation states that clients must ignore unknown fields
- Look for JSON schema settings like additionalProperties: false that enforce strict parsing
baseContent.exampleComment: The API documentation should state that clients must ignore unknown fields. This enables adding fields without version bumps.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Strict parsing rejects unknown fields
const schema = z.object({ name: z.string() }).strict();

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// Passthrough allows unknown fields for forward compatibility
const schema = z.object({ name: z.string() }).passthrough();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design clients to ignore unknown fields. Document this as a contract requirement.

items.text: Can enum/status values be extended without breaking existing clients?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Adding new values to an enum (e.g., order statuses) can break clients that do exhaustive matching. Clients should handle unknown values gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If a client's switch/case on a status field doesn't have a default branch, a new status value causes runtime errors. This makes enum extension a hidden breaking change.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that API documentation warns clients to handle unknown enum values
- Verify client SDKs include a default/fallback for enum parsing
- Look for changes that add new enum values and assess client impact
baseContent.exampleComment: Adding the 'on_hold' status will break clients that exhaustively match statuses without a default case. Document that new statuses may be added at any time.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document that enums are open (new values may appear) and ensure clients have default handlers.

items.text: Does pagination remain stable across versions with consistent cursor/offset behavior?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pagination cursors or offset-based pagination should work consistently across API versions. Changing pagination semantics breaks long-running data syncs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clients that paginate through large datasets rely on stable cursor behavior. Changing how cursors work mid-pagination causes missed or duplicated records.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that pagination parameters (page, limit, cursor, after) have the same semantics
- Verify cursor formats are backward-compatible
- Confirm total counts and page metadata are consistent
baseContent.exampleComment: The v2 endpoint uses cursor-based pagination, but v1 used offset-based. Clients migrating mid-sync will lose their position. Support both during the transition.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep pagination semantics stable across versions. If changing strategies, support both during migration.

items.text: Does the API support content negotiation for format evolution?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Content negotiation via Accept and Content-Type headers lets clients request specific response formats. This enables adding new formats (e.g., JSON→Protobuf) without breaking existing clients.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without content negotiation, format changes require new endpoints or version bumps. Proper negotiation enables transparent format evolution.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the API respects Accept headers
- Verify unsupported Accept types return 406 Not Acceptable
- Confirm the default content type is documented
baseContent.exampleComment: The API returns JSON regardless of the Accept header. Respecting content negotiation now future-proofs the API for format additions like Protobuf or MessagePack.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Support content negotiation to enable transparent format evolution without versioning changes.

items.text: Is there a comprehensive changelog documenting all changes between versions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A changelog lists all additions, changes, deprecations, and removals between API versions. It should be machine-parseable (e.g., following Keep a Changelog format) and published with each release.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a changelog, consumers can't efficiently assess the impact of upgrading. They resort to diffing documentation or trial-and-error testing.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that a changelog exists and covers the current release
- Verify it categorizes changes (added, changed, deprecated, removed)
- Confirm breaking changes are clearly highlighted
baseContent.exampleComment: This version bump adds 3 new endpoints and deprecates 2, but the changelog only mentions the new endpoints. Document the deprecations as well.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Maintain a detailed changelog for every version. Categorize and highlight breaking changes.

items.text: Is the OpenAPI/Swagger specification updated to reflect version changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The OpenAPI specification is the single source of truth for the API contract. It must be updated atomically with code changes to stay accurate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stale specs cause incorrect SDK generation, misleading documentation, and broken contract tests. The spec and implementation should always match.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the OpenAPI spec diff matches the code changes
- Verify new fields/endpoints are documented in the spec
- Confirm deprecated endpoints are marked with deprecated: true in the spec
baseContent.exampleComment: The new 'metadata' field was added to the handler response, but the OpenAPI spec still shows the old schema. Update the spec to include the new field.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
# Spec doesn't match implementation
User:
  properties:
    name:
      type: string
    # missing: email field that's actually returned

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
User:
  properties:
    name:
      type: string
    email:
      type: string
      description: Added in v2.1

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Update the OpenAPI spec in the same PR as the code change. Treat spec drift as a bug.

items.text: Is there a clear matrix showing which features are available in which versions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A version matrix is a table showing which endpoints, fields, and features exist in each supported API version. It helps consumers understand what's available at a glance.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When multiple versions are active, consumers need a quick reference to see which version has the features they need. Without a matrix, they have to read multiple changelogs.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the API documentation includes a version comparison matrix
- Verify it's updated when features are added or deprecated
- Confirm it clearly indicates which versions are supported vs. sunset
baseContent.exampleComment: With v1, v2, and v3 all active, consumers have no quick way to compare feature availability. Add a version matrix to the documentation landing page.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Maintain a version matrix when multiple API versions are simultaneously supported.

items.text: Are deprecations prominently visible in API documentation, not buried in footnotes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deprecated endpoints and fields should be clearly marked in the API documentation with visual indicators (strikethrough, warning banners) and sunset dates.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If deprecation notices are buried in changelogs or footnotes, developers integrating with the API won't notice them until the endpoint is removed.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that deprecated endpoints have visual deprecation markers in docs
- Verify deprecated fields are annotated in schema documentation
- Confirm the sunset date is visible on the endpoint's documentation page
baseContent.exampleComment: The /v1/search endpoint is deprecated but its documentation page has no warning banner. Add a prominent deprecation notice with the sunset date and migration link.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Make deprecations impossible to miss in documentation. Use banners, not footnotes.

items.text: Does the version numbering follow semantic versioning rules?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) communicates the nature of changes: MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for backward-compatible additions, PATCH for bug fixes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent versioning lets consumers automate update decisions. If a MINOR version contains breaking changes, automated dependency updates can cause production outages.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Verify MAJOR is bumped for breaking changes
- Confirm MINOR is bumped for new features (backward-compatible)
- Check that PATCH is used only for bug fixes
- Look for breaking changes in MINOR or PATCH bumps
baseContent.exampleComment: This release removes a field (breaking change) but only bumps the MINOR version. This should be a MAJOR version bump per semantic versioning rules.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow semantic versioning strictly. Breaking changes always require a MAJOR version bump.
